name: ezi-gex
description: Unicode aware regex engine for Zig тАФ runtime and comptime. Thompson NFA: linear-time, ReDoS-safe; Literal Prefilter; Backtrack; Lazy Dfa; Eager Dfa; Full \p{} Unicode properties, named captures, case folding, pluggable backends. Zero allocation per match. WASM-compatible.
readme:
# ezi_gex

A Unicode-aware regex engine for Zig that runs at **runtime and `comptime`**, with
a **pluggable backend** architecture.

- **Linear-time.** Thompson-NFA based тАФ no catastrophic backtracking, ever.
  `(a*)*b` on a long input is fine.
- **Unicode-first.** `\w`, `\b`, `\p{L}`, `\p{Script=Greek}`, case folding, and
  classes are all Unicode-correct. Character **classes** are resolved once into
  sorted code-point ranges тАФ matched by a range check, with **no per-character table
  lookup** at match time; `\b` and `\X` consult `ezi_code`'s property tables directly.
  Unicode comes from [`ezi_code`](https://github.com/shaik-abdul-thouhid/ezi-code);
  ezi_gex never touches `std.unicode`.
- **Comptime-capable.** Compile a pattern *and run the match* at compile time тАФ
  the program lands in `ro_data`, the matcher runs in `comptime`. (The C++ `ctre`
  trick, in Zig, with full Unicode.)
- **Pluggable.** Matching lives behind a small, vtable-free **backend contract**.
  The library ships seven backends and a dispatcher; you can write your own and
  drop it into the same front door. See [`docs/architecture.md`](docs/architecture.md)
  for the contract, and the step-by-step
  [*write your own backend*](docs/usage-guide.md#8-writing-your-own-backend) walkthrough
  in the usage guide.
- **Target-agnostic.** Pure computation over caller-provided memory тАФ no syscalls,
  no global allocator, no platform assumptions in the library code. Imports and
  compiles anywhere Zig (plus `ezi_code`) does: native, `wasm32-freestanding` /
  `wasm32-wasi`, and bare-metal `*-freestanding` (verified to compile for all four).

## Status

**`0.4.0-dev` on `main`** тАФ the active development branch. The latest *tagged* release is
**`v0.3.1`**, but it predates most of the work below; for the current engine, track `main`
(see [Installing](#installing)). Pre-1.0, so the API may still change, but everything in the
public surface is annotated `@stable-since: vX.Y.Z` and is covered by SemVer. Tracks a recent
Zig dev build (`0.17.0-dev`); it will not compile on stable 0.16.

**What works is tested** тАФ **360 tests** pass (per-module behaviour; cross-backend
conformance, a wide differential corpus where every backend must agree with the Pike VM,
runtime + comptime parity; and a dedicated **ReDoS-immunity suite**, `engine/redos.zig`,
proving the engine is quadratic-immune).

The default `auto` engine is **byte-DFA-first**, with the **eager DFA**
(`engine/backends/edfa.zig`) as the primary span engine: it **fully determinizes the byte
automaton at build time** into a frozen, **Hopcroft-minimized** `states ├Ч byte_classes`
table, so the matcher is a bare table walk with **no per-search state** and runs at
**comptime as well as runtime** (the genuine CTRE-lane тАФ `auto` bakes a real frozen DFA into
`ro_data` for tiny patterns at comptime, while a big Unicode class stays on the Pike VM at
comptime but still gets the eager DFA at runtime). Its `find` is **O(input) on every
pattern**, by a static build-time strategy (`program.prone`): an accepting consuming loop
(`\w+`, `\d+`) gets a greedy **anchored restart**; a *prone* pattern (e.g. `\w+@\w+`'s pre-`@`
run) gets the **reverse-DFA two-pass**. The **lazy DFA** (`engine/backends/dfa.zig`) is the
**fallback** when the eager table overflows its state bound. Both are leftmost-first and
conformance-proven against the Pike VM.

Landed across `0.4.0-dev` (all on `main`):

- **`\b`/`\B` word boundaries on the byte DFAs** тАФ previously the worst benchmark outlier
  (stuck on the Pike VM, ~10├Ч behind). Distributed: the **eager DFA** bakes **ASCII** word
  boundaries into the frozen table (zero match-time decode); the **lazy DFA** handles full
  **Unicode** boundaries via a *decode-hybrid* (decode only at boundary positions). `auto`
  routes by a cached whole-input ASCII check (ASCII тЖТ eager, non-ASCII тЖТ lazy), staying
  correct for every input. (`\bthe\b` on Sherlock went from ~89 MiB/s to multi-GiB/s.)
- **`(?m)` line anchors on the eager DFA** тАФ `(?m)^`/`(?m)$` run on the DFA (anchored restart
  with line context) for non-prone patterns; a prone `(?m)` falls to the linear Pike VM.
- **One-pass capture fast path** (`backends.onepass`) тАФ a single deterministic thread fills
  captures in O(input) for provably one-pass patterns (`(\d{4})-(\d{2})-(\d{2})`,
  `(\w+)@(\w+)`); `auto` uses it for the anchored capture fill after a DFA locates the span.
- **Hopcroft/Moore minimization** of the eager DFA (results-invariant; shrinks the auxiliary
  tables, e.g. `\w+@\w+`'s reverse DFA ~3├Ч fewer states).
- **`$`/`\z` (`text_end`) on the lazy DFA** тАФ closing the last eager/lazy capability gap.
- **Whole-run literal `memmem` prefilter** in `auto` тАФ the leading-literal start-skip now
  leaps on the *whole* literal run (`\bthe\b` jumps "the"тЖТ"the", not 't'тЖТ't'), via a SIMD
  `memchr` on the run's rarest byte + inline verify. See *Performance*.

`0.2.0`/`0.3.0` foundations still in place: full case folding, grapheme `\X`, a two-tier
`Options` (semantic + results-invariant strategy), `(?x)` verbose mode, ASCII mode,
dead-on-invalid UTF-8, and the **byte-NFA lowering + `ByteMap` equivalence classes**
(`engine/byte.zig`) тАФ the zero-decode UTF-8 automaton substrate the DFAs determinize.

## Installing

The latest **tagged** release is `v0.3.1`. It predates the `0.4.0-dev` work above
(`\b`/`(?m)` on the DFAs, the one-pass capture path, DFA minimization, the whole-run
prefilter) тАФ for those, **track `main`** (below). Via git ref (resolves the tag at fetch time):

```sh
zig fetch --save git+https://github.com/shaik-abdul-thouhid/ezi-gex.git#v0.3.1
```

Or via plain HTTP tarball (pins the content hash in `build.zig.zon`):

```sh
zig fetch --save https://github.com/shaik-abdul-thouhid/ezi-gex/archive/refs/tags/v0.3.1.tar.gz
```

**Tracking `main` (unreleased `0.4.0-dev`)** тАФ if you want the latest, in-development
surface before it's tagged, fetch the branch instead of a tag. This resolves `main`'s
current commit and pins its hash in `build.zig.zon`; re-run it to move up:

```sh
zig fetch --save git+https://github.com/shaik-abdul-thouhid/ezi-gex.git#main
```

`main` is the development branch: it builds and is tested, but APIs there are not yet
covered by a tag, so they can still change before `0.4.0`. For reproducible builds,
prefer a tagged release; reach for `main` only when you specifically need unreleased work.

Then in `build.zig` (the `ezi_code` dependency is resolved transitively тАФ you only
add `ezi_gex`):

```zig
const ezi_gex = b.dependency("ezi_gex", .{ .target = target, .optimize = optimize });
exe.root_module.addImport("ezi_gex", ezi_gex.module("ezi_gex"));
```

## Quick look

> For the full hands-on tour тАФ every op, the pipeline from lexing, comptime paths,
> and writing your own backend тАФ see [`docs/usage-guide.md`](docs/usage-guide.md).

```zig
const gex = @import("ezi_gex");

// тФАтФА runtime: compile a (possibly user-supplied) pattern; never crashes тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФА
var diag: gex.Diagnostic = .{};
var re = gex.compileRuntime(gpa, "(?<user>\\w+)@(?<host>\\w+)", &diag, .{}) catch {
    // diag.message() + diag.faultySlice(pattern) tell you what and where.
    return;
};
defer re.deinit();

// The Scratch is the per-search working state тАФ you own it; one per thread.
// Build it directly off the backend's `Scratch` type (heap-backed here); the front
// door never constructs it for you. Reuse one across many searches; never share a
// Scratch across threads.
var sc = try @TypeOf(re).Scratch.init(gpa, &re.program);
defer sc.deinit(gpa);

if (re.find(&sc, "ping bob@example")) |m| {
    _ = m.slice("ping bob@example"); // "bob@example"
}

// captures: provide a slots buffer of re.slotCount() == 2*(groups+1)
const slots = try gpa.alloc(?usize, re.slotCount());
defer gpa.free(slots);
if (re.captures(&sc, slots, "bob@example")) |c| {
    _ = c.namedSlice("user"); // "bob"
    _ = c.namedSlice("host"); // "example"
}

// тФАтФА comptime: program baked into the binary; match runs at compile time тФАтФАтФАтФАтФА
const Re = comptime gex.compileComptime("(\\d{4})-(\\d{2})", .{});
const yes = comptime Re.isMatchComptime("y2026-06"); // true, computed at build
const cap = comptime Re.capturesComptime("y2026-06").?; // groups resolved at build (usable from v0.2.0-dev)
const year = comptime cap.groupSlice(1).?;              // "2026", a ro_data slice (usable from v0.2.0-dev)
```

> ### тЪая╕П Comptime has limits тАФ and the trade-off is yours
>
> `compileComptime` runs the **entire** parse тЖТ HIR тЖТ program lowering inside the Zig
> compiler's const-evaluator and bakes the result into `ro_data`. Two consequences you own
> as a deliberate choice тАФ the library will not decide them for you:
>
> 1. **It only works until the compiler runs out of room.** Const-eval is bounded by the
>    eval-branch quota and compiler memory; a large, deeply-nested, or pathological pattern
>    can blow the quota or make builds slow and memory-heavy. **Prefer `compileRuntime` for
>    big or user-supplied patterns** тАФ runtime compilation has no such ceiling.
> 2. **Each comptime program adds *its* ranges to `ro_data`.** A `compileComptime` regex
>    bakes its class ranges in: ~6.3 KB per *distinct* `\w` (~800 ranges), ~5.3 KB per
>    `\p{L}`, тЙд0.5 KB for ASCII classes. **Identical classes within a pattern are interned
>    to one range-block**, so a counted repeat like `\w{3,32}` costs *one* `\w`, not one
>    per copy тАФ repetition no longer multiplies the table. The cost is one block per
>    *distinct* class per comptime pattern; `compileRuntime` adds nothing to the binary.
>    The Unicode *tables* themselves are a fixed one-time cost, **not** per-pattern тАФ see
>    [┬з Binary size](#binary-size). Stacking many *different* Unicode classes across many
>    comptime programs is the only thing that grows `ro_data`; check the delta then.

## Matching & captures

Everything below uses the default `auto` engine тАФ `compileRuntime` /
`compileComptime`. The shape never changes: **compile once тЖТ make a `Scratch` тЖТ
run searches**. The compiled regex is immutable and shareable; the `Scratch` is the
single piece of mutable per-search state.

> Prefer copy-paste? The usage guide has a runnable recipe for each op below in
> [┬з3 Front-door recipes](docs/usage-guide.md#3-front-door-recipes), plus the
> [`Options`](docs/usage-guide.md#options) reference and the
> [comptime / no-allocator](docs/usage-guide.md#4-comptime--no-allocator-usage) paths.

### 1. The `Scratch` тАФ the engine only needs *a* scratch

The engine is **`Scratch`-type agnostic.** Every search op takes a
`*@TypeOf(re).Scratch`, and that is the *entire* requirement. The front door never
constructs it, never stores an allocator for it, and assumes **nothing** about what it
holds тАФ whether a `Scratch` is heap-allocated, carved from a caller buffer, stateless
(`struct{}`), or something exotic is **purely the backend's design**. Buffer
semantics, allocator semantics, comptime-ability: all optional, all the backend's
call. `Compiled` holds only the `Scratch` *type* and forwards your `&sc` straight
through to the backend тАФ so you build the `Scratch` yourself, directly off
`@TypeOf(re).Scratch`, threading in `&re.program`:

```zig
var re = try gex.compileRuntime(gpa, "[a-z]+\\d+", &diag, .{});
defer re.deinit();

// Heap-backed тАФ every built-in backend's Scratch defines `init` / `deinit`.
var sc = try @TypeOf(re).Scratch.init(gpa, &re.program);
defer sc.deinit(gpa);
```

If the backend implements the **buffer convention** (its `Scratch` exposes
`Buf` / `bufferLen` / `initBuffer` тАФ every built-in except the runtime-only lazy `dfa`
does), you can hand it caller-owned storage instead, with no allocator and no allocation
*during* a search:

```zig
// Fixed buffer тАФ `bufferLen` reports how many `Buf` words this program needs.
const buf = try gpa.alloc(@TypeOf(re).Scratch.Buf, @TypeOf(re).Scratch.bufferLen(&re.program));
defer gpa.free(buf);
var sc_buf = try @TypeOf(re).Scratch.initBuffer(buf, &re.program);

// For a comptime regex the length is comptime-known тЖТ a stack array, no allocator:
const Re = comptime gex.compileComptime("[a-z]+\\d+", .{});
var stack_buf: [@TypeOf(Re).Scratch.bufferLen(&Re.program)]@TypeOf(Re).Scratch.Buf = undefined;
var sc_ct = try @TypeOf(Re).Scratch.initBuffer(&stack_buf, &Re.program);
```

A backend with a different construction protocol is built however *it* specifies тАФ for
a stateless one that is simply `var sc: @TypeOf(re).Scratch = .{};`. Whatever the
backend's choice, you end up with a value the engine accepts.

> The front door dictates no representation and reaches for no scratch method on the
> runtime path: it cares that a `Scratch` *value* exists, not how it was made. (The
> comptime helpers тАФ `isMatchComptime`/`findComptime`/тАж тАФ are the one exception: with
> no allocator in const-eval they carve a buffer `Scratch` inline, so there they do
> require the backend's buffer convention.)

### 2. Searching тАФ `isMatch`, `find`, `findAll`, `count`, `split`

```zig
var re = try gex.compileRuntime(gpa, "\\w+", &diag, .{});
defer re.deinit();
var sc = try @TypeOf(re).Scratch.init(gpa, &re.program);
defer sc.deinit(gpa);

const text = "the quick brown fox";

_ = re.isMatch(&sc, text);                 // true
const m = re.find(&sc, text).?;            // first match (leftmost)
_ = m.start;  _ = m.end;  _ = m.slice(text); // "the"

_ = re.count(&sc, text);                   // 4 тАФ non-overlapping matches

var it = re.findAll(&sc, text);            // iterate every match
while (it.next()) |hit| _ = hit.slice(text); // "the","quick","brown","fox"

var parts = re.split(&sc, "a, b ,c");      // split on the pattern
while (parts.next()) |piece| _ = piece;    // "a, b ,c" split on /\w+/ тЖТ punctuation/spaces
```

Matching is **leftmost-first** (Perl/JS), linear-time, and Unicode-correct. `find`
returns `null` (not an error) when there is no match.

### 3. Captures тАФ numbered and named groups

`captures` resolves the first match's submatches into a `slots` buffer you provide.
Size it with `re.slotCount()` (`== 2 * (groups + 1)` тАФ two offsets per group plus the
whole match at index 0). At **runtime** the group count is dynamic, so allocate; at
**comptime** it is known, so a stack array works.

```zig
var re = try gex.compileRuntime(gpa, "(?<user>\\w+)@(?<host>\\w+)", &diag, .{});
defer re.deinit();
var sc = try @TypeOf(re).Scratch.init(gpa, &re.program);
defer sc.deinit(gpa);

const slots = try gpa.alloc(?usize, re.slotCount()); // 2 * (2 groups + 1) = 6
defer gpa.free(slots);

if (re.captures(&sc, slots, "ping bob@example")) |c| {
    _ = c.match().slice("ping bob@example"); // "bob@example"  (group 0)
    _ = c.groupSlice(1).?;                    // "bob"          (by number)
    _ = c.namedSlice("user").?;               // "bob"          (by name)
    _ = c.namedSlice("host").?;               // "example"
    _ = c.group(2);                           // ?Match for group 2, or null if absent
}
```

A group that did not participate reads back `null` (`groupSlice`/`namedSlice` return
`null`, never stale data). To stream captures over every match, use `capturesAll`
(it reuses one `slots` buffer тАФ each `Captures` is valid only until the next
`next()`):

```zig
var cit = re.capturesAll(&sc, slots, "a@b x@y");
while (cit.next()) |c| _ = c.namedSlice("user"); // "a", then "x"
```

### 4. Replace тАФ `replaceAll` with `$0`/`$1`/`$name` templates

```zig
var re = try gex.compileRuntime(gpa, "(\\w+)@(\\w+)", &diag, .{});
defer re.deinit();
var sc = try @TypeOf(re).Scratch.init(gpa, &re.program);
defer sc.deinit(gpa);
const slots = try gpa.alloc(?usize, re.slotCount());
defer gpa.free(slots);

var out: std.Io.Writer.Allocating = .init(gpa);
defer out.deinit();
try re.replaceAll(&sc, "bob@example", "$2/$1", slots, &out.writer); // "example/bob"
```

### 5. The same calls at comptime

When the whole regex *and* the input are known at compile time, the match runs in
const-eval тАФ no `Scratch` to manage, no allocator. The results are baked into
`ro_data` and are usable at runtime as well:

```zig
const Re = comptime gex.compileComptime("(\\d{4})-(\\d{2})", .{});

const ok   = comptime Re.isMatchComptime("y2026-06"); // true
const m    = comptime Re.findComptime("y2026-06").?;  // whole match
const n    = comptime Re.countComptime("2026 2027");  // 2
const c    = comptime Re.capturesComptime("y2026-06").?;
const year = comptime c.groupSlice(1).?;              // "2026" тАФ by number
```

**Comptime named captures.** Named groups resolve at compile time exactly like at
runtime тАФ `namedSlice` works inside const-eval, and the result is a `ro_data` slice
you can keep as a plain `const`:

```zig
const Re = comptime gex.compileComptime("(?<year>\\d{4})-(?<month>\\d{2})", .{});

if (comptime Re.capturesComptime("y2026-06")) |c| {
    const year  = comptime c.namedSlice("year").?;  // "2026"
    const month = comptime c.namedSlice("month").?; // "06"
    _ = year;  _ = month;
}

// тАжor pull one straight out as a compile-time constant baked into the binary:
const year = comptime Re.capturesComptime("y2026-06").?.namedSlice("year").?; // "2026"
```

## Supported syntax

| Category | Supported |
|---|---|
| Literals, `.`, `\|`, `*` `+` `?` `{m,n}`, lazy `*?`тАж | тЬЕ |
| Groups `(тАж)`, `(?:тАж)`, named `(?<n>тАж)`/`(?P<n>тАж)` | тЬЕ |
| Classes `[...]`, `[^...]`, ranges, `\d \w \s` (+ negations) | тЬЕ |
| Unicode `\p{L}` `\P{тАж}` `\p{Script=тАж}`, `\pL` | тЬЕ |
| Anchors `^ $ \A \z`, word boundary `\b \B`, multiline `(?m)` | тЬЕ |
| Inline flags `(?i)` `(?m)` `(?s)` `(?x)`, scoped `(?i:тАж)` | тЬЕ |
| Escapes `\n \t \xHH \x{тАж} \u{тАж} \cX`, comments `(?#тАж)`, verbose `(?x)` | тЬЕ |
| `\X` grapheme cluster (UAX #29) | тЬЕ (matched by `backtrack`/`auto`) |
| Backreferences, lookaround, atomic/conditional, recursion, `\QтАж\E` | тЭМ rejected with a precise error |

Anchors are JS/RE2-style: `$` without `(?m)` is end-of-input (`\z`), and `\Z` is
treated as `\z`. See [`docs/architecture.md`](docs/architecture.md) ┬зCaveats.

## Backends

| Backend | Strategy | Captures | Comptime | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `auto` *(default)* | dispatches the others | тЬЕ | тЬЕ | just use this |
| `pikevm` | breadth-first NFA | тЬЕ | тЬЕ | general, large inputs; Unicode `\b` |
| `backtrack` | bounded depth-first NFA | тЬЕ | тЬЕ | small inputs; the only `\X` backend |
| `literal` | substring / literal-alternation | whole-match | тЬЕ | pure-literal patterns |
| `onepass` | single deterministic NFA thread | тЬЕ | тЬЕ | provably one-pass capture fill (anchored) |
| `bytepike` | byte-stepping Pike VM (zero-decode) | тЬЕ | тЬЕ | byte-automaton substrate; ASCII `\b`, no `\X` |
| `edfa` *(default span engine)* | **eager** DFA тАФ frozen `states ├Ч byte_classes` table | span-only | тЬЕ | fast O(n) span scan; ASCII `\b`, `(?m)`, `$`/`\z` |
| `dfa` | lazy DFA over the byte automaton (cached transitions) | span-only | тЬЧ (runtime-only) | fallback when the eager table overflows; Unicode `\b` |

`compileRuntime`/`compileComptime` use `auto`, which **prefers the eager DFA** (`edfa`) for
the span scan, falls back to the lazy `dfa` when the eager table overflows its state bound,
then to the NFA. Captures are filled **anchored at the DFA span** by `onepass` (for one-pass
patterns) or the Pike VM. `auto` routes feature by feature and **never `@compileError`s** тАФ it
is correct for every pattern and input: ASCII `\b` and non-prone `(?m)` ride the **eager** DFA,
**Unicode `\b`** (non-ASCII input) the **lazy** DFA, and `\X` / a prone `\b` or `(?m)` / a mixed
`$` stay on the code-point Pike VM. The DFA is **on by default** (`byte_engine = .auto`/
`.enabled`); `.disabled` opts back to the NFA-only program. Force a specific backend with the
`*With` variants: `gex.compileRuntimeWith(gex.backends.pikevm, gpa, pat, &diag, .{})`.

The usage guide covers [choosing a backend](docs/usage-guide.md#choosing-a-specific-backend)
and walks the whole [*write your own backend*](docs/usage-guide.md#8-writing-your-own-backend)
process end to end.

## When to use comptime тАФ honest advice

The library has two things it calls "comptime" and they behave quite differently, so
it's worth being clear about which one you actually want.

**`compileComptime` тАФ program in ro_data, match at runtime.** The full compile pipeline
runs in the Zig const-evaluator and the result is baked into the binary. Matching still
happens at runtime over runtime input. You use it exactly like a `compileRuntime` regex
once compiled.

**`*Comptime` methods (`isMatchComptime`, `findComptime`, `capturesComptime`, тАж) тАФ both
pattern and input are compile-time constants.** The entire match runs during the build
and the result is a compile-time constant. These are a different beast.

### For most code: just use `compileRuntime`

Pattern compilation is a one-time cost тАФ microseconds to low milliseconds, paid once at
startup and never again. There is no meaningful performance argument for `compileComptime`
over `compileRuntime` followed by runtime matching. The compiled regex is immutable; you
compile once, store it somewhere, and reuse it for the lifetime of the process.

`compileRuntime` also has cleaner error handling (a `Diagnostic` with a caret, not a
`@compileError` buried in a stack of const-eval frames), no binary-size overhead, and no
compiler eval-quota ceiling. It should be your default for nearly everything тАФ fixed
patterns included.

### When `compileComptime` is actually worth it

Use it when **all three** are true:

1. The pattern is fixed in source (not user-supplied, not assembled at runtime).
2. You specifically want the build-time guarantee тАФ a typo in the pattern is a
   `@compileError`, not a runtime error path you have to handle.
3. The pattern is light on Unicode classes.

That third one is the catch тАФ though a much smaller one than it used to be. This engine
resolves Unicode classes to sorted code-point ranges at HIR time, and a
`compileComptime` program bakes its ranges into `ro_data`. One `\w` is ~6.3 KB
(802 ranges ├Ч 8 B), `\p{L}` ~5.3 KB, `\d` ~0.5 KB, `\s` ~80 B. **Identical classes
inside a pattern are interned to a single range-block**, so a counted repeat like
`\w{3,32}` costs *one* `\w` (~6.3 KB), not one per copy тАФ repetition no longer
multiplies the table. The remaining cost is one block per *distinct* class per
comptime pattern. If your pattern is ASCII-heavy (`\d`, `[a-z]`, explicit ranges) the
cost is negligible; if it stacks many *different* Unicode classes, check the delta.
See [┬з Binary size](#binary-size) for the whole picture (the Unicode tables themselves
are a fixed one-time cost, not a per-pattern one).

### When the `*Comptime` match methods make sense

These require the input to be a compile-time constant, which makes them self-selecting:
if your data is dynamic, you simply cannot use them. That rules out essentially all
production matching code.

They genuinely shine as build-system tools: asserting at build time that a constant
string matches an expected format (`comptime assert(Re.isMatchComptime(EXPECTED))`),
generating a lookup table from a constant corpus, or pulling a named group out of a
source-level constant as its own typed constant. The results are baked in and cost
nothing at runtime. Just keep the input short тАФ the eval-branch quota scales with input
length, and a large string in const-eval will slow your build.

## Thread-safety

The `Program` is `*const` during a search тАФ compiler-enforced immutable, so a single
`Compiled`/`Program` is freely shareable across threads with no locks, no atomics, no
global state. The `Scratch` is the only mutable per-search state, and **how it behaves
under concurrency is the backend's choice** тАФ a backend is free to make its `Scratch`
thread-safe. The built-in backends do **not**: their `Scratch` is mutated on every
search, so give **each thread its own** (never pool one across threads).

Two facts about the built-ins, both stemming from the *caller-supplied* allocator
rather than any hidden internal one тАФ the front door allocates nothing during a search:

- The `backtrack` heap `Scratch` (which `auto` uses for small inputs) grows its visited
  set on demand **through the allocator you passed to `Scratch.init`**. So if several
  threads' scratches share one *non-thread-safe* allocator, two growing at once race
  inside that allocator тАФ give each thread its own allocator, or a thread-safe one.
- A **buffer-backed `Scratch`** (`initBuffer`) and the **`pikevm`** backend allocate
  nothing at all while matching, sidestepping that entirely.

Full details in [`docs/architecture.md`](docs/architecture.md) ┬з11 and the usage guide's
[┬з9 Thread-safety](docs/usage-guide.md#9-thread-safety).

## Performance

Honest about where it stands. **Tier 1 тАФ the literal / prefilter fast path тАФ is now
wired:**

1. The `literal` backend scans with `std.mem.indexOf`: SIMD `memchr` to locate a
   one-byte needle, BoyerтАУMooreтАУHorspool with a skip table for longer ones тАФ instead
   of an `eql` at every byte position. On memchr-friendly needles that is **~20├Ч** the
   old position-by-position scan; it is never slower.
2. `auto` consumes the HIR [`Analysis`](docs/usage-guide.md#7-the-analysis-prefilter-facts)
   on NFA *and* DFA patterns: when every match must begin
   with a fixed literal, the **whole leading literal run** drives a SIMD `memmem`-style skip
   that leaps straight to each candidate start тАФ literal-to-literal, not byte-to-byte
   (`\bthe\b` jumps "the"тЖТ"the" instead of 't'тЖТ't', skipping every interior "the"). The skip
   is a SIMD `memchr` (`std.mem.indexOfScalarPos`, `@Vector`) for the run's *rarest* byte plus
   a cheap inline `eql` verify тАФ deliberately **not** `std.mem.indexOfPos`, which falls back to
   a non-SIMD scan for needles тЙд 4 bytes (the common "the"/"http" sizes). Each candidate is
   confirmed with an *anchored* run; a `^`/`\A` pattern
   skips the leftward scan entirely; the **rarest required byte** drives a sound
   fast-reject (no `@` in the input тЗТ no `\w+@\w+` match, return at once); and a
   min-length gate rejects inputs too short to hold any match. Every `Analysis` fact is a
   sound one-sided bound, so the prefilter never drops a real match тАФ it only avoids
   running the engine where one cannot start. Toggle with `strategy.prefilter`.

**Tier 3 тАФ the byte DFA тАФ is wired and now ON BY DEFAULT, with the *eager* DFA as the
primary span engine** (`backends/edfa.zig`, reached through `auto`). `auto` builds the eager
DFA тАФ a fully-determinized, **Hopcroft-minimized** frozen `states ├Ч byte_classes` table тАФ and
uses it for `isMatch`/`find` on most patterns, including **ASCII `\b`** and non-prone **`(?m)`**
line anchors and trailing **`$`/`\z`**; only `\X`, a prone `\b`/`(?m)`, or a mixed `$` fall to
other arms. Captures are filled **anchored at the DFA span** by `onepass` (one-pass patterns)
or the Pike VM. The lazy DFA (`backends/dfa.zig`) is the **fallback** тАФ when the eager table
overflows its state bound, and the arm that carries **Unicode `\b`** (decode-hybrid) on
non-ASCII input. The default is **at Rust parity (~1.1├ЧтАУ1.3├Ч) on every class scan** and **тЙе the
code-point Pike VM in every cell** (5тАУ10├Ч on character-class scans), it is **results-invariant**
(`conformance.zig` pins span and captures to the Pike VM and fuzzes the strategy knobs), and
**quadratic-immune** (`engine/redos.zig`). `strategy.byte_engine = .disabled` opts back to the
compact NFA-only program.

**`find` is O(n) on every pattern тАФ via a build-time strategy choice.** `computeProne`
detects, at build, whether the anchored DFA has a **non-accepting cycle reachable from a
start** (it can consume an unbounded run without ever accepting):

- **Non-prone** (`\w+`, `\d+`, `[A-Za-z]+` тАФ the consuming loop is itself accepting) тЖТ
  **anchored restart**: one greedy frozen-table walk per match. O(input) because no start
  can scan far without hitting an accepting state (~1.1 GiB/s).
- **Prone** (`\w+@\w+` on long word runs, `[ab]*c`) тЖТ the **reverse-DFA two-pass**: a
  forward one-pass (`utrans` `.*?`-prefix table) locates the match **end**, a frozen
  **reverse** DFA (determinized at comptime *and* runtime) the leftmost **start**. O(input),
  replacing the old ╬Ш(n┬▓) anchored restart on this begins-everywhere-completes-rarely class.

There is **no per-search probing** тАФ the arm is fixed at build. A **trailing-`$`** pattern
(`\w+@\w+$`, `[ab]*c$`, `\w+$`: every match ends at input end) takes a third O(input) arm тАФ
the end is pinned to `input.len`, so a single **reverse-DFA pass from the end** finds the
leftmost start (no anchored restart, which would be ╬Ш(n┬▓) on these begin-but-don't-complete
shapes). The eager DFA also **builds only the tables it will use**: `utrans` and the reverse
table are built **only for prone or trailing-`$` patterns**, so a non-prone `\w+` stores just
its forward `trans` table (~141 KB) instead of all three (~1 MB). Leftmost-first,
conformance-pinned to the Pike VM.

Since first writing this, several items here have **landed**: `\b`/`\B` and `(?m)` on the
byte DFAs, a one-pass capture backend (`onepass`), DFA **Hopcroft minimization**, `$`/`\z`
on the lazy DFA, and the whole-run literal `memmem` start-skip (item 2 above).

**Still open (additive, no API change):** a **multi-substring (Teddy / Aho-Corasick)
prefilter** for literal alternations тАФ the single biggest remaining gap vs. Rust тАФ and a
**sparse DFA table encoding** to shrink the (already minimized) Unicode-class eager tables.
See [`docs/architecture.md`](docs/architecture.md) ┬з10 for the tier roadmap.

## Binary size

Class **matching** is delegated to `ezi_code`'s **enumerable range tables** тАФ the HIR
resolves every class (`\d \w \s`, `\p{тАж}`, scripts, `[...]`) to sorted code-point ranges,
matched by a range check with **no per-character table lookup**. The Unicode **assertions**
are the exception, and contribute the bulk of the linked Unicode data: match-time `\b`/`\B`
(Unicode) tests word-ness via `ezi_code`'s **DerivedCoreProperties** table, `\X` via the
**grapheme-break** table, and full `(?i)` folding via the **case-fold** tables тАФ so those are
linked too (`ezi_gex` still links none of `ezi_code`'s per-code-point *General_Category* trie).

Either way the cost is **fixed, not growing** тАФ the tables are shared and linked once, the same
whether you compile 1 pattern or 10 000:

| What | Cost | Grows withтАж? |
|---|---|---|
| `ezi_code` Unicode tables linked into the demo тАФ class **ranges** (`category_runs`, `derived_runs`, `script_runs`) **plus** the assertion **tables** (DerivedCoreProperties for `\b`, grapheme-break for `\X`, case-fold for `(?i)`) | **тЙИ 380 KB, linked once** *(measured by symbol span; largest: DerivedCoreProperties ~161 KB, case-fold ~96 KB, category ranges ~49 KB, derived ranges ~40 KB)* | **nothing** тАФ same for 1 pattern or 10 000 |
| a runtime-compiled regex (`compileRuntime`) | on the **heap**, not the binary | the pattern |
| a comptime-compiled regex (`compileComptime`), per pattern | one program in `ro_data`; ~6.3 KB per *distinct* `\w`, ~5.3 KB per `\p{L}`, тЙд0.5 KB for ASCII classes | the pattern's distinct classes |

So once a program touches the regex engine at all, the Unicode tables are paid for **once** and
never again тАФ adding more patterns, longer patterns, or more Unicode classes cannot push that
~380 KB any higher (and a program that uses *no* Unicode assertions and only ASCII classes pulls
far less). The only size that scales with your code is `ro_data` for the `compileComptime`
programs you choose to bake in, and even there **identical classes are interned** (a class used N
times in one pattern is stored once; `\w{3,32}` costs one `\w`, not 35). `compileRuntime` adds
nothing to the binary beyond the shared tables.

> **The byte engine** (on by default; `byte_engine = .disabled` opts out) builds a
> separate *byte* automaton on the **heap** тАФ never linked into the binary тАФ and it is
> larger than the code-point program because a Unicode class becomes automaton structure
> rather than a flat table. Two passes keep it bounded: **UTF-8 suffix sharing** (a
> `(lo,hi,next)` cache that emits each class's shared continuation tails once) and
> **single-copy `x+`** compilation. Measured: `\w+` is ~3.9 k instructions (down from
> ~11 k), `\w+@\w+` ~7.9 k (down from ~23 k); ASCII patterns are unchanged. A
> `byteWorthLowering` cost-gate keeps a pathological pattern (a big class repeated dozens
> of times) on the compact code-point engine entirely. The **lazy** DFA (the fallback)
> never builds the whole automaton тАФ it materializes only the states an input visits (a
> handful over ordinary text), so the NFA's bulk is build-time, not per-search, memory.
> The **eager** DFA (`backends.edfa`, the default span engine) *does* freeze the whole DFA
> into a `states ├Ч byte_classes` table тАФ into `ro_data` at comptime тАФ and is also the
> CTRE/bake-it-in path: tiny for literal/ASCII patterns (`abc` is 5 states / ~100 B), a
> few hundred states for a Unicode class. It now **builds only the tables it uses** (a
> non-prone `\w+` keeps just its forward table, ~141 KB, not the forward + `.*?`-prefix +
> reverse trio, ~1 MB). See [`docs/architecture.md`](docs/architecture.md) тЖТ *The byte
> substrate* / *The eager DFA*.
>
> **Build-time note тАФ determinization is ~O(states) (hash-interned).** The forward and reverse
> determinizers intern DFA states through an open-addressing hash, so building a *big Unicode
> class* DFA is ~linear in its state count. (Was an O(states┬▓) scan тАФ `\w+@\w+`, `\w+@\w+$`,
> `\p{L}+$` took ~seconds to *compile*; now milliseconds.) It is a **one-time build cost; match
> time is O(input), unaffected.** ASCII-class and literal patterns build instantly.

Reference point тАФ the bundled `main.zig` demo (which exercises runtime *and* comptime
compilation, classes, captures, replace, split, `\p{L}`, `\p{Script=тАж}`, and all three byte
backends), built with Zig `0.17.0-dev` on macOS arm64:

| Optimize mode | Demo binary |
|---|---|
| `Debug` | **~3.4 MB** (3,574,728 B) |
| `ReleaseSafe` | **~1.27 MB** (1,329,416 B) |
| `ReleaseFast` | **~1.14 MB** (1,191,160 B) |
| `ReleaseSmall` | **~758 KB** (775,992 B) |

The bulk of the `Debug` figure is the Zig `Debug` runtime (DWARF self-unwind, UBSan, the
allocator), not regex data тАФ `ReleaseSmall` strips that down to ~758 KB, of which the shared
`ezi_code` Unicode tables (~380 KB, above) are the largest single contributor. Your own binary
will be smaller still: it won't link the demo's spread of backends and Unicode features, and
`compileRuntime` adds nothing beyond the shared tables.

## Documentation

- [`docs/usage-guide.md`](docs/usage-guide.md) тАФ **the hands-on guide**: copy-paste
  recipes for every front-door op, the full pipeline used **from lexing** (scan тЖТ AST
  тЖТ HIR тЖТ backend), comptime/no-allocator paths, and a complete, runnable, step-by-step
  **"write your own backend"** walkthrough. Start here if you want to *do* something.
- [`docs/architecture.md`](docs/architecture.md) тАФ architecture, data flow,
  **how to write your own backend** (with a complete tiny example), caveats, and
  the implicit assumptions backends rely on.
- [`src/core/README.md`](src/core/README.md) тАФ the frontend (scanner тЖТ AST тЖТ HIR).
- [`src/engine/README.md`](src/engine/README.md) тАФ the contract, the NFA, the
  backends, the front door, and a backend quickstart.
- [`src/engine/backends/README.md`](src/engine/backends/README.md) тАФ the built-in
  backends (incl. `bytepike`/`dfa`/`edfa`) and how `auto` chooses.

## License

MIT тАФ see [LICENSE](LICENSE).

releases.name: v0.3.1
releases.body:
Patch release: fixes a quadratic-time (O(n^2)) ReDoS in the **default engine**.

- **Fixed** тАФ `auto`'s prefilter confirmed anchored at *every* prefix-byte occurrence, so a begin-but-don't-complete pattern with a dense prefix (e.g. `a+b` on `aaaaтАжa!`) ran in **╬Ш(n┬▓)** тАФ ~1.1 s at 64 KiB тАФ even though the eager DFA's own find is O(n). It now routes such programs (`prone`/`end_anchored`) to that O(n) native find (`a+b`: **1.1 s тЖТ 186 ┬╡s**, now linear). Same fix on the lazy-DFA and NFA arms. Results-invariant.
- **Added** тАФ a ReDoS-immunity test suite (`engine/redos.zig`) with deterministic guards (`backtrack.Scratch.steps`, `auto.Scratch.confirm_probes`) that pin linear work *and* correct arm-routing, plus a `redos` benchmark тАФ the bench that caught this.
- **Docs** тАФ corrected `backtrack.zig`'s recursion-depth claim (stack depth is input-proportional; the bare backtracker is a bounded-input tool, shielded on the default path).

Additive and results-invariant тАФ no public signature or match-result changes. Full analysis in [CHANGELOG.md](https://github.com/shaik-abdul-thouhid/ezi-gex/blob/v0.3.1/CHANGELOG.md).

releases.name: v0.3.0
releases.body:
The byte **DFA is now the default span engine**, and matching is **linear-time / ReDoS-immune on every supported pattern**.

## Highlights

- **Eager DFA (`backends.edfa`) тАФ the default span engine.** Fully determinizes the byte automaton at build into a frozen `states ├Ч byte_classes` table; **stateless**, **comptime-bakeable** (the CTRE lane), `O(input)` on every pattern. ~5тАУ10├Ч the old NFA default on character-class scans тАФ **at Rust-`regex` parity** (~1.1тАУ1.3├Ч).
- **`$`/`\z` quadratic immunity.** Trailing-`$` patterns (`\w+$`, `[ab]*c$`, `\w+@\w+$`, and `$`-in-every-branch alternations like `foo$|bar$`) match in `O(input)` via a reverse-DFA-from-end pass тАФ never a ╬Ш(n┬▓) anchored restart. A *mixed* `$` (`a$|b`) is routed to the linear Pike VM.
- **Hash-interned determinization.** DFA-state interning is now an open-addressing hash (was `O(states┬▓)`), so big Unicode-class patterns compile in ~ms instead of ~seconds. Build-time only; no semantic change.
- **Lazy DFA fallback** (`backends.dfa`) with an `O(n)` reverse-DFA `find`, for patterns past the eager DFA's state bound.
- **Prefilter wired** (leading-literal `memchr` + rarest-required-byte fast-reject), and the **literal-alternation ╬Ш(n┬▓) fixed** (single SIMD `indexOfAny` pass).

Spans and captures stay leftmost-first and conformance-pinned to the Pike VM (**311 tests**, runtime + comptime). Depends on `ezi_code` 0.4.1; minimum Zig `0.17.0-dev`.

Full notes: [CHANGELOG.md](https://github.com/shaik-abdul-thouhid/ezi-gex/blob/v0.3.0/CHANGELOG.md).

releases.name: v0.2.0 тАФ byte-NFA lowering + ByteMap
releases.body:
Second tagged release. Pre-1.0 (SemVer; APIs annotated `@stable-since`). Requires a recent Zig `0.17.0-dev` build.

## Headline: the byte-engine substrate

- **Byte-NFA lowering + `ByteMap` equivalence classes** (`engine/byte.zig`) тАФ the zero-decode UTF-8 automaton substrate the lazy DFA will determinize. A scalar range lowers to UTF-8 byte-range sequences (the Cox/RE2 `utf8-ranges` algorithm, exact across length + surrogate boundaries), so a Unicode class matches the same code points with **no decode**. `byteClasses()` computes sound, contiguous byte equivalence classes (alphabet compression тАФ even `\w+`'s automaton collapses to ~112 classes). Builds at comptime and runtime; gated by `byteLowerable(hir)` (`\X` and `\b`/`\B` are not byte-lowerable).
- **`bytepike` backend** тАФ a byte-stepping Pike VM that executes the byte program (captures, comptime + runtime). The reference executor proving the lowering correct: conformance shows it agrees with `pikevm`/`backtrack`/`auto` across the whole case table, runtime and comptime. It is **not** `auto`'s default (per-byte stepping is not a throughput win тАФ the DFA, coming in 0.3.0, is what the substrate exists for). Invalid UTF-8 is dead-on-invalid by construction.

> **Memory note:** byte programs are *larger* than the code-point program for Unicode classes (a single `\w+` is ~137 KB vs 6.5 KB; ASCII patterns are unchanged) тАФ inherent to a byte NFA, and only paid when the byte path is used. The huge NFA still collapses to ~100 byte classes, which is the point: the lazy DFA keys on class id, so its alphabet stays tiny.

## Also in 0.2.0

- **Full case folding** (`CaseFold.full`) тАФ `(?i)├Я` also matches `ss`, `ямА` matches `ff`, etc. (literals; classes stay simple-fold).
- **Grapheme `\X`** (UAX #29) тАФ matched by the `backtrack`/`auto` backends.
- **Two-tier `Options`** тАФ semantic flags (`case_fold`, `case_insensitive`/`multiline`/`dot_matches_newline` seeding, `unicode` ASCII mode) + a results-invariant `strategy` tier.
- **`(?x)` verbose mode**, **`SearchOptions.span_end`**, reserved **`Match.pattern`** (multi-pattern API), and the **`utils` facade** as the sole `ezi_code` seam.
- **Dead-on-invalid UTF-8** in input тАФ a match never spans a malformed byte (behaviour change vs 0.1.0's `U+FFFD` substitution). Patterns stay strictly validated.

See `CHANGELOG.md` for the full list.

## Install

```sh
zig fetch --save git+https://github.com/shaik-abdul-thouhid/ezi-gex.git#v0.2.0
```

```zig
const ezi_gex = b.dependency("ezi_gex", .{ .target = target, .optimize = optimize });
exe.root_module.addImport("ezi_gex", ezi_gex.module("ezi_gex"));
```

**Next (`0.3.0-dev`):** the lazy DFA over this byte substrate.

releases.name: v0.1.0 тАФ first tagged release
releases.body:
First public surface and first tagged release of **ezi_gex** тАФ a Unicode-aware regex engine for Zig that runs at **runtime and comptime**, behind a pluggable, vtable-free backend contract.

### Highlights
- **Pipeline** `pattern тЖТ AST тЖТ HIR тЖТ Program тЖТ match`, runnable at runtime (heap) and comptime (program baked into `ro_data`, match runs in const-eval).
- **Backends:** `auto` (default dispatcher), `pikevm` (Pike VM, captures, linear-time), `backtrack` (bounded, memoized, linear-time), `literal` (substring / literal-alternation).
- **Tier-1 prefilter wired (new):** the `literal` backend scans with `std.mem.indexOf` (SIMD `memchr` + BoyerтАУMooreтАУHorspool) тАФ ~20├Ч on memchr-friendly needles, never slower; and `auto` consumes the HIR `Analysis` on NFA patterns тАФ a leading-literal first-byte `memchr` prefilter, a `^`/`\A` start short-circuit, and a `min_utf8_len` length gate. Every fact is a sound one-sided bound, so no real match is ever dropped.
- **Unicode-first:** classes, scripts, `\w`/`\b`, and simple case folding resolve to code-point ranges with **zero Unicode-table lookups at match time** (via [`ezi_code`](https://github.com/shaik-abdul-thouhid/ezi-code); `ezi_gex` never touches `std.unicode`).
- **Target-agnostic library:** pure computation over caller-provided memory тАФ verified to compile for `wasm32-freestanding`, `wasm32-wasi`, `riscv64-freestanding`, and `aarch64-linux`.

Everything in the public surface is annotated `@stable-since: v0.1.0` and is covered by SemVer from this tag on. Requires a recent Zig `0.17.0-dev` build.

### Install
```sh
zig fetch --save git+https://github.com/shaik-abdul-thouhid/ezi-gex.git#v0.1.0
# or, plain HTTP tarball (pins the content hash):
zig fetch --save https://github.com/shaik-abdul-thouhid/ezi-gex/archive/refs/tags/v0.1.0.tar.gz
```

See [CHANGELOG.md](https://github.com/shaik-abdul-thouhid/ezi-gex/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md) for the full list.
